summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the common problem of neglecting hard drive defragmentation by showing how to automate the process using Windows XP's built-in tools. He reveals that XP includes a lesser-known command-line defrag.exe utility that can be combined with the task scheduler for hands-off maintenance. The post provides step-by-step instructions for setting up scheduled defragmentation tasks and includes a Windows Script Host file for defragmenting multiple drives simultaneously. He also recommends SysInternals' PageDefrag for handling locked system files that can't be defragmented while Windows is running.
key_points: Windows XP includes a command-line defrag.exe utility that most users don't know about
The task scheduler can automate defragmentation to run during idle times
A simple WSH script can defragment all fixed drives in sequence
Some system files like registry hives require boot-time defragmentation with specialized tools
Built-in free tools are often 'good enough' compared to commercial alternatives
money_quotes: I tend to ignore defragmenting my hard drive until I belatedly realize it probably looks like swiss cheese by now.
Wouldn't it be nice if the operating system took care of defragmentation all by itself in the background when I'm not using the computer? Ah, to dream.
I'm sure commercial disk defragmenters do a better job, but this one's good enough – and it's free!
Some crucial system files, such as the registry hive, are always locked while the OS is running.
key_insight: Automating routine maintenance tasks with built-in OS tools eliminates the burden of remembering to do them manually.
